content:
# Hebrews Chapter 2 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

### v.6 — "What is man" vs. "What are mere mortals"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "What is man that you remember him, or the son of man that you care for him?" | ti estin anthrōpos (Ps 8:4) |
| LOGOS | "What is man, that you are mindful of him, or the son of man, that you care for him?" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "What are mere mortals that you should think about them, or a son of man that you should care for him?" | "Mere mortals" for anthrōpos; pluralizes |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. anthrōpos is singular "man" — a generic singular referring to humankind. PNEUMA adds "mere" (not in Greek) and pluralizes. The author quotes Psalm 8 to set up the Christological reading in v.9 where "man/son of man" becomes Jesus specifically. Pluralizing weakens this individual application.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "mere" and pluralizes.

---

### v.10 — "author/founder of their salvation" vs. "a perfect leader, fit to bring them into their salvation"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to make the author of their salvation complete through sufferings" | ton archēgon tēs sōtērias autōn dia pathēmatōn teleiōsai |
| LOGOS | "should make the founder of their salvation perfect through suffering" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "it was only right that he should make Jesus, through his suffering, a perfect leader, fit to bring them into their salvation" | Adds "Jesus"; "perfect leader" splits teleioō + archēgos; adds "fit to bring them into" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. archēgon tēs sōtērias = "founder/pioneer of their salvation." PNEUMA splits this into "a perfect leader, fit to bring them into their salvation" — adding a purpose clause and changing "founder" to "leader." The Greek says God made the pioneer of salvation perfect through suffering — one action, one result. PNEUMA fragments it into two.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA fragments and adds.

---

### v.11 — "all have one source" vs. "have the same Father"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "are all of one" | ex henos pantes |
| LOGOS | "all have one source" | "Source" — implied |
| PNEUMA | "have the same Father" | Specifies "one" as "Father" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. ex henos pantes — "all from one." The "one" is deliberately unspecified: it could mean one Father, one source, one origin, one nature. LOGOS adds "source" to complete the English sense. PNEUMA specifies "Father" — a reasonable interpretation but not the only one. The ambiguity may be intentional.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA specifies what the author leaves open.

---

### v.14 — "destroy the one who has the power of death" vs. "break the power of the devil"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that through death he might destroy the one having the power of death, that is, the devil" | katargēsē ton to kratos echonta tou thanatou, tout' estin ton diabolon |
| LOGOS | "that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "only by dying could he break the power of the devil, who had the power of death" | Restructures; "break the power of" for katargeō |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. katargeō means "to render inoperative/destroy/abolish" (same verb as 2 Tim 1:10). The object is the devil himself — not his power. PNEUMA changes the object from the devil to his power, and changes the verb from "destroy" to "break the power of." The author's claim is more radical: Christ destroyed the devil, not merely curtailed his power.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA shifts object from devil to his power.

---

### v.17 — "to make propitiation" vs. "offer a sacrifice that would take away the sins"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to make propitiation for the sins of the people" | hilaskesthai tas hamartias tou laou |
| LOGOS | "to make propitiation for the sins of the people" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "he could offer a sacrifice that would take away the sins of the people" | "Offer a sacrifice that would take away" for hilaskesthai |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. hilaskesthai means "to make propitiation/atonement" — a single priestly action. PNEUMA expands to "offer a sacrifice that would take away" — adding both the means ("sacrifice") and the result ("take away"). The author uses the verb alone, without specifying the mechanism. The high priest theme will be developed later; here the verb stands on its own.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA expands a single verb into means and result.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive expansion | 2 | Fragments archēgos + teleioō (v.10), expands hilaskesthai (v.17) |
| PNEUMA specifies the ambiguous | 1 | "Father" for "one" (v.11) |
| PNEUMA shifts objects | 1 | Devil's power for the devil himself (v.14) |
| PNEUMA adds diminishing terms | 1 | "Mere mortals" for anthrōpos (v.6) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 2's most significant disagreement is v.14 — whether Christ destroyed the devil or broke his power. The author's verb (katargeō) and object (ton diabolon) are clear: the devil himself is rendered inoperative. PNEUMA's softening to "break the power of" is common in dynamic translations but weakens the author's radical claim. The v.11 ambiguity ("all of one") is also notable — the author may deliberately leave the "one" unspecified to encompass multiple meanings.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
